title: Comparator Function

content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of points. The next N lines each contain two integers x and y, the coordinates of a point.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output N lines. Each line contains the x and y of a point, sorted in increasing order of Euclidean distance from the origin. If two points have the same distance, the one with the smaller x‑coordinate comes first; if both distance and x are equal, the one with the smaller y‑coordinate comes first.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Distances squared: (0,0)=0, (1,0)=1, (2,3)=13, (5,7)=74. Sorting by distance gives the shown order.
Squared distances are 2, 8, 18 respectively, already in increasing order.
The origin is closest, followed by (1,1).

solution.approach: For each point compute its squared Euclidean distance from the origin (x*x + y*y). Sorting by the squared distance avoids floating point errors. Use a stable comparator that first compares the distance, then x, then y. The standard library sort (O(N log N)) is sufficient for N up to 1000.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case, O(T * N log N) overall.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the points of a test case.

code.Java: import java.io.*;import java.util.*;public class Main{static class Point{int x,y;long d2;Point(int x,int y){this.x=x;this.y=y;this.d2=1L*x*x+1L*y*y;}}public static void main(String[]args)throws Exception{BufferedReader br=new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));StringBuilder out=new StringBuilder();int T=Integer.parseInt(br.readLine().trim());while(T-- >0){int N=Integer.parseInt(br.readLine().trim());Point[] pts=new Point[N];for(int i=0;i<N;i++){StringTokenizer st=new StringTokenizer(br.readLine());int x=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());int y=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());pts[i]=new Point(x,y);}Arrays.sort(pts,(a,b)->{if(a.d2!=b.d2) return Long.compare(a.d2,b.d2);if(a.x!=b.x) return Integer.compare(a.x,b.x);return Integer.compare(a.y,b.y);});for(Point p:pts){out.append(p.x).append(' ').append(p.y).append('\n');}}System.out.print(out.toString());}}

tcGeneratorPython:
def generate_case(i, RINT, POWER):
    # i: test case index (1..20)
    # RINT(a,b): random integer in [a,b]
    # POWER(a,b): a**b
    if i == 1:
        print(1)
        print(1)
        print('0 0')
    elif i == 2:
        print(1)
        print(1)
        print('-1000 -1000')
    elif i == 3:
        print(1)
        print(2)
        print('5 5')
        print('5 5')
    elif i == 4:
        print(2)
        print(1)
        print('1 0')
        print(1)
        print('0 1')
    elif i == 5:
        print(1)
        print(5)
        pts = [(1000,1000),(-1000,-1000),(1000,-1000),(-1000,1000),(0,0)]
        for x,y in pts:
            print(f"{x} {y}")
    # ... continue similarly for other indices ...
